act1.morning_prayer:
Urumqi. Early morning. Winter presses cold through the window.

Your name is Aysha. You work at a textile factory. Your husband Abdurahman was a teacher — but is no longer permitted to teach in Uyghur. Your son Yusuf is seven.
act1.prayer_choice:
Prayer time. Curtains drawn. On every corner, a camera — what the authorities call the 'Smart City System.' Its real name: IJOP. Integrated Joint Operations Platform.

What do you do?
act1.quiet_prayer:
Prostrating, you think: 'Turning toward God in my own home has become something that might be considered a crime.'

The prayer is short and silent. But whole.
act1.skip_prayer: A weight settles on your shoulders. You set something down — small, but real. Loss begins this way: small, constant, necessary.
act1.phone_check: Your phone. The government-mandated 'Jingwang' app is installed — it grants authorities full inspection rights. Everything except WeChat and state-approved apps is blocked.
act1.phone_choice_prompt: Your relatives abroad are still in your contacts. Your aunt in Kazakhstan. A university friend in Istanbul. If they find these in the next inspection, there will be questions.
act1.choice_keep_contacts: Keep them — deleting them feels like losing them
act1.deleted_contacts:
The progress bar fills. Faces, voices — gone as data. But you still remember.

Is memory dangerous now too?
act1.kept_contacts: You kept them. You know the risk — but some connections can't be cut.
act1.street_scene:
The street. Facial recognition cameras every 50 meters. By 2022, Urumqi has over 100,000 cameras — one of the densest surveillance networks in the world per capita.

Walking, you feel your footsteps. Ordinary. You want to be invisible.
act1.checkpoint_narration: A checkpoint. Every day, sometimes multiple times. They scanned your data. Scanned your face. You wear a wristband with 'State' written on it — marking you as Uyghur.
act1.comply_result:
You pass through. The soldier watches until you're around the corner.

This gaze has been the same for six years. At first you felt rage. Now only exhaustion.
act1.ask_reason_result:
The soldier paused. 'Asking for reasons raises suspicion.' He writes something on a form.

Your name was noted somewhere. You don't know what it says. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything.
act1.school_scene:
Yusuf's school. The sign is in Mandarin — no Uyghur. Since 2017, Uyghur-language education has been banned in state schools.

Yusuf goes inside. He won't learn in your language.
act1.child_language: Mama, what's 'wolf' in Uyghur? The teacher didn't say.
act1.language_choice_prompt: Speaking Uyghur at home is now officially restricted. But this moment is yours — you can still give your child his language.
act1.choice_only_mandarin: Just tell him in Mandarin — safer
act1.teach_uyghur:
'Böri,' you said quietly. 'It means wolf.'

His eyes lit up. This small word — your language, your identity, your mother's voice. No one can erase this yet.
act1.only_mandarin:
'Láng,' you said. Mandarin.

Yusuf repeated it. Correct pronunciation. You could have taught him in your mother tongue. But not today.
act1.evening_scene:
Evening. Abdurahman comes home — silent. That familiar expression on his face. Something else happened today. You don't ask — not asking is sometimes a form of protection.

The three of you at the table. Yusuf hums a song — Mandarin words. But the melody is Uyghur.
act1.act1_end:
Uyghur identity is not illegal in this land. But each day it becomes a little harder. Language, prayer, face — all are data. All are tracked.

What will you dream tonight?

act2.knock_at_door:
2:14 AM. A knock.

A knock at this hour means one thing.
act2.door_choice_prompt: On the bookshelf there's a Quran. On your phone, there may be foreign numbers you didn't delete. If it's the 'committee,' they'll search everything.
act2.choice_hide_quran: Hide the Quran first, then open
act2.hide_quran:
You put it in the kitchen cabinet, behind a pot. Fifteen seconds. Your hands are shaking.

Because you're hiding your scripture in your own home.
act2.officer_speaks: Neighborhood committee inspection. We're coming in. Phones, books, personal items. Resistance is an administrative offense.
act2.search_scene:
Three people entered. They separated Abdurahman into a corner. You stayed in the kitchen. Yusuf is sleeping — you don't want him to wake.

You make no sound. You know what making a sound means.
act2.ijop_reveal:
They scanned your devices. Cross-referenced with facial recognition. Checked the IJOP system.

This system —
act2.search_result:
The search ended. The officers leave but say to Abdurahman: 'Be at the education center tomorrow morning.'

'Education center.' That's the official name.
act2.father_taken:
Morning came. Abdurahman left — no suitcase, alone. No return date.

His last look was at you. At Yusuf. Then he turned and walked.
act2.father_taken_react: Do the neighbors know anything? Or do you just sit and wait?
act2.neighbor_whispers:
My husband left six months ago too. 'Education,' they said. We spoke once — the phone is monitored, everything recorded. 'I'm fine, I'm learning,' he said. His voice wasn't.

Some come back. But those who return come back different.
act2.stay_silent:
You sit. You wait. When Yusuf wakes he'll ask 'Where is Baba?'

What will you say?
act2.camp_truth:
The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, 2022 report: 'Serious human rights violations' in Xinjiang facilities. Estimated: over 1 million detentions.

Is Abdurahman among them?
act2.camp_network_reveal: Who benefits from not knowing this? Who profits from this system?
act2.decision_point:
Staying, waiting — maybe Abdurahman comes back.

Leaving — maybe you reach safety, maybe you find him.

Neither is certain. Both are fear.
act2.act2_end: Life in East Turkestan is built on this dilemma: is staying complicit in oppression, is leaving an act of abandonment?

act3.escape_plan:
You decided. Take Yusuf, go. Abdurahman told you something once: 'If you ever have to leave — go north. The Kazakhstan border.'

Yusuf is seven. How much does he understand?
act3.route_choice_prompt: Two routes. Which is less dangerous — you don't know. But you know that staying still is also a choice.
act3.choice_route_kazakhstan: North: mountain pass toward the Kazakhstan border
act3.choice_route_south: South: toward India, longer but with refugee camps
act3.route_kazakhstan:
The northern route. The Tian Shan mountains. A guide was found — an old shepherd. He asked for money. Six months of savings. All of it.

Yusuf carries a small backpack. Inside: one photograph. His father and him. Last winter.
act3.route_south:
The southern route. Longer, harder. The smugglers aren't trustworthy but there's no other option.

Yusuf didn't ask where we're going. As if he understood — that asking would serve no purpose.
act3.mountain_crossing:
The mountain. Walking at night. Yusuf is asleep — you carry him on your shoulder, heavy. Your feet are freezing. The guide is silent, fast.

Below, Chinese territory. Above, something else — you don't know what yet.
act3.fellow_traveler:
At the pass, another family. A woman, two children. No husband — he also went to the 'education center.' They're fleeing like you.

Can you trust them?
act3.travel_together:
You walked together. Her name is Gulnar. Her children talked with Yusuf — in whispers. In Uyghur. Their own language.

Walking, you realized: fleeing alone and fleeing together are not the same thing. Together is heavier, but more real.
act3.travel_alone: You parted ways. You move faster but you can't stop looking back — where are that woman and her children now?
act3.border_moment:
The border line. A wire fence, a watchtower on a hill, and beyond — Kazakhstan.

The tower's light didn't find you. The guide said 'run now.'

You ran.
act3.language_memory:
On the Kazakhstani side, safe, you sit on the ground.

Yusuf is asleep — his head on your shoulder. He's murmuring in his sleep. 'Böri,' he says. Wolf.

When you taught him that word, you didn't teach it for this moment. But here, tonight, this word is here with you.
act3.act3_end:
━━━ CHAPTER COMPLETE ━━━

Uyghurs who have fled East Turkestan number over 300,000 as of 2023. Most live as refugees in Turkey, Kazakhstan, and Europe.

Those who remain — millions — are still there.

For those like Abdurahman, international law has yet to produce a concrete enforcement mechanism.

Open the archive.
